mnemonics.title: Virtual Cluster Components
mnemonics.content: HANG: Hypervisor, Application layer, Network virtualization, Guest OS - the four pillars of virtual cluster architecture.

mnemonics.title: Resource Management Goals
mnemonics.content: SLAP: Scalability, Load balancing, Availability, Performance - the four pillars of effective virtual cluster management.

mnemonics.title: VM Placement Strategies
mnemonics.content: BIRD: Best Fit Decreasing, First Fit, Randomized, First Fit Decreasing - common bin packing variants for VM placement.

mnemonics.title: Live Migration Requirements
mnemonics.content: NSSS: Network connectivity, Shared storage, Sufficient memory, Same CPU features (unless EPT enabled) - essential for seamless live migration.

acronyms.usage: VMware's automatic load balancing that migrates VMs between hosts based on resource demands.
Architecture where memory access time depends on memory location relative to CPU; critical for VM placement decisions.
Contract defining guaranteed performance metrics (uptime, response time, throughput) that must be met.
Metric measuring energy efficiency: PUE = Total Facility Energy / IT Equipment Energy. Lower is better.
Bin packing algorithm sorting items descending before placement; used in VM consolidation.
Mechanisms to guarantee performance characteristics like bandwidth, latency, and jitter for VMs.

visualTips.visualization: Draw three physical servers at bottom, each divided into 3 VM slices using colored rectangles. Connect all VMs to a common virtual network switch at top. Label the physical layer 'Hardware', middle as 'Hypervisor', top as 'Virtual Infrastructure'.

Show bins (servers) as rectangles with capacity labels. Place VM rectangles of varying sizes into bins, showing remaining capacity. Color-code bins to show utilization percentage (green>70%, yellow 40-70%, red<40%).

Draw a 2-socket server with CPU1 and CPU2 connected to separate memory banks. Place VMs entirely in one quadrant to show local vs remote memory access paths. Use arrow thickness to represent access latency.
Show a physical server with labeled capacity: 16 cores, 64GB RAM. Stack virtual resources above: 64 vCPUs (4:1), 96GB virtual memory (1.5:1). Use dashed lines to show overcommitment ratios.

analogies.analogy: Think of a virtual cluster like a office building. Physical servers are floors, the hypervisor is the building management, and VMs are individual offices. Each office (VM) has its own locked door (isolation), but they share common areas (physical resources).

Like an airline overbooking flights. Not all passengers (VMs) use their full allocated seat (resources) simultaneously. Airlines succeed because not everyone shows up - similarly, VMs rarely use peak resources at the same time.
Like moving a patient from one hospital bed to another while on life support. The equipment (VM state) is continuously transferred, with only a brief pause during the final switchover.
Like a hotel moving furniture between rooms based on occupancy. The balloon driver is like hotel staff redistributing furniture from empty rooms to occupied ones needing more furniture.
